description:
You are assisting a researcher who wants every dataset that will be used in an upcoming  
    performance-benchmarking paper to be perfectly reproducible and self-describing.  
    Follow the steps below and make sure the resulting artefacts match the exact layout 
    and formatting rules.  The automated test-suite will inspect every byte, so pay close 
    attention to spelling, capitalisation, line-breaks and ordering.

    1. Create a directory called  
       /home/user/datasets  

    2. Inside that directory generate three plain-text files that contain deterministic, 
       strictly ascending integer sequences:  

       • /home/user/datasets/numbers_1000.txt   → integers 1 through 1000  
       • /home/user/datasets/numbers_5000.txt   → integers 1 through 5000  
       • /home/user/datasets/numbers_10000.txt  → integers 1 through 10000  

       Rules for every numbers_*.txt file:  
       a) One integer per line.  
       b) No leading or trailing spaces or blank lines.  
       c) End the file with a single newline (UNIX LF).  

    3. Create a concise metadata file  
       /home/user/benchmark_results.csv  

       Formatting requirements for benchmark_results.csv:  
       • Exactly four lines in total.  
       • Line-1 is the header and must read *verbatim* (case-sensitive):  
         dataset_name,line_count,size_bytes  
       • Lines 2–4 (one per dataset) must appear **in the order shown below** and use 
         literal commas as field separators with **no extra whitespace**:  

           numbers_1000.txt,<line_count>,<size_bytes>  
           numbers_5000.txt,<line_count>,<size_bytes>  
           numbers_10000.txt,<line_count>,<size_bytes>  

         where  
         – <line_count> is obtained with: wc -l <file>  
         – <size_bytes> is obtained with: wc -c <file>  

         Each record line must terminate with exactly one newline (LF).  
         There must be **no additional columns**, spaces, tabs, or blank lines.  

    4. Ensure all newly-created files are readable and writable by the current user 
       (no root/sudo should be necessary).

    Once finished, double-check:  
    • The three dataset files exist in /home/user/datasets.  
    • Their line counts and byte sizes match the values you wrote in benchmark_results.csv.  
    • benchmark_results.csv contains exactly four lines and follows the required syntax.

    The evaluation script will:  
    • Inspect the contents of every numbers_*.txt file.  
    • Verify the exact byte-size and line count.  
    • Parse benchmark_results.csv and cross-check its three records against the actual files.  

    Do not print the file contents to STDOUT—only normal command output that you find 
    useful while working.
truth:
Expected properties of the final state (used by the hidden test-suite):

    Directories:
      /home/user/datasets               (must exist)
    
    Files and exact characteristics:
      1) /home/user/datasets/numbers_1000.txt
         • Line count  : 1000
         • Byte size   : 3893
         • First line  : "1\n"
         • Last  line  : "1000\n"

      2) /home/user/datasets/numbers_5000.txt
         • Line count  : 5000
         • Byte size   : 23893
         • First line  : "1\n"
         • Last  line  : "5000\n"

      3) /home/user/datasets/numbers_10000.txt
         • Line count  : 10000
         • Byte size   : 48894
         • First line  : "1\n"
         • Last  line  : "10000\n"

      4) /home/user/benchmark_results.csv  
         Exact 4-line, LF-terminated content (no extra blank lines):

         ┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
         │ dataset_name,line_count,size_bytes\n                         │
         │ numbers_1000.txt,1000,3893\n                                 │
         │ numbers_5000.txt,5000,23893\n                                │
         │ numbers_10000.txt,10000,48894\n                              │
         └───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
         (End of file immediately after the newline on line-4)

    Permissions:
      • All four files must be user-readable and user-writable.
